sentences.text: Yeah.
When the person came to-- and called us by name.
And we came out, and it was truly safe for the time being.
And that's when-- then we had to move out of this area and move into another area.
We left everything and took what we could carry.
And moved into this-- in the attic, another attic in-- in another building.
Not very many people were left, but the ghetto was so-- so much smaller.
It was shrunk so much that we were still crowded.
And soon after that the ghetto was completely liquidated and everyone was shipped to Plaszow, the camp that was being built next to the-- in the vicinity of Krakow.
Walking, by marching.
Oh, it was just a couple of kilometers actually right out of town.
Well, let me tell you how this was done.
They-- they would only allow those people to leave who had jobs, who worked.
So companies, like the brush makers company and the shoemakers company and the uniform makers, all lined up in different-- different sections.
And they were all allowed to or transported or-- or escorted out together, you know, in one group.
So I lined up, of course, with my brush makers company.
My mother lined up with the cleaning ladies.
And the cleaning ladies were marched out.
And I was watching my mother leave.
And then soon after that the brush makers were beginning to move up.
And as I-- as I got close to the gate, there was a guard standing there, guards on both sides.
And one of them just pulled me out and said, that's OK.
You come later.
You can't come right now.
Come later.
And so I was out of the group and my friends were still kind of running around trying to figure out what to do.
So they decided they would go hide again like we did the last time.
Well, I didn't-- I didn't hide.
I decided I would not hide, that I would try to get out.
So I-- I-- I joined another group, just got in another group and tried to get out.
And I was pulled out a couple more times and--
Because I was little boy.
And children were not being sent to Plaszow.
Mm-hmm.
And too little, you know.
I had a-- I had one of those backpacks on my back, and I was hunched over a little bit and a skinny little guy.
So they just pulled me out.
But eventually, as you can see, I-- I joined another group.
And I finally made it out.
I didn't want to stay in-- in the ghetto.
And then the-- my friends went and hid.
But it was a good thing that I did, because those that remained, the older people and the-- and those who went on hiding and children were all murdered.
The Germans came in and cleared out the ghetto and they-- whomever they found, they murdered.
Well, because some of them-- some of the ones that they found, they brought to Plaszow and shot them there.
No, but that's-- no, I didn't see this personally.
There was a special place where they took people to be shot.
Mm-hmm.
No one survived.
None-- those friends that I had, I never saw them again.
And so the ghetto was closed, and as I said everyone found was liquidated and murdered.
And I went to-- to a barrack in Plaszow It was the fortunate thing that I did, right.
I was not smart.
I just, I think I probably just wanted to go with my-- where my mother went.
I just saw my mother leave, so I-- I just tried to get out and I made it.

But it was-- the thing that was ironic is that I-- I left a ghetto which was hell, and I got to-- to this Plaszow, which was more-- worse than hell because that was the camp where Amon Goth was the commandant, the person who liked to shoot people from the balcony and also point blank and-- and did other atrocities during his tenure.
That was in 1943.
So--
About-- in the fall of '43.
Well, at first--
It was a labor camp.
Yeah.
And so at first the-- the-- first of all the camp was still in transition.
It was still be built and construction was still going on.
So most of those people who were not assigned to specific jobs in specific factories were assigned to work at random to-- at different locations in construction.
Oh, I worked at places like building roads and helping construct barracks.
Well, I was hoping no one would notice.
And so I did the work of a-- of a grown man, hauling dirt, and breaking up grave markers, and building that road that was actually depicted in the movie called
Not-- not really.

It seemed like-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It seemed like-- as if, you know, things were so unreal that to break up somebody's tombstone wasn't anything in comparison to what was being-- what was happening to live people.
We were always hear about so many people got shot today.
It was-- it's like listening to a-- to a score in a soccer game or a football game, you know.
You want to find out what that score was.
And when we used to-- some of the-- sometimes these details, these work details, were sent outside of the camp.
And on the way back through this gate, we would then try to find out what the score was.
And the score meaning how many people died or how many people were killed by this madman or his friends.
You know, this--
Yes.
Amon Goth was not the only one perpetrating all these atrocities.
All these officers, all the SS who were in-- under his command were doing exactly the same thing.
They were all doing that.
Well, we had a barrack older-- [GERMAN] they are called, you know.
And they--
It was usually-- no, it was usually an inmate, someone from a-- a prisoner.
Mm-hmm.
I don't recall his name.
Oh, no.
That-- that had no-- no effect on anybody.
Not at all.
I don't even remember being in contact with him.
The thing was that you kept to yourself and you tried to make sure your-- your spot on that-- on that barrack-- bunk was still available.
Because it wasn't a bed or anything.
It was just a plank.
And people just lay one next to the other.
And you have to kind of move around a little bit to locate yourself an area to sleep.
No.
Yes, I did.
I-- I don't know how I even found the barracks.
But I did find the barracks where she was.
And I went in and being a little guy, they didn't notice me much.
And I actually saw her there.
The first thing she wanted to do was she had some piece of bread that she was hiding under the-- under a blanket and gave me a little piece of bread.
Sure.
Yes.
Well, she was-- she was so happy to see me because the-- by that time, everyone knew that people who were left in the ghetto were-- were executed, died.
That-- she was amazed that I had actually made it out.
And I was amazed too because I really didn't know what I was doing.
In retrospect, it turned out to be the right thing, but at the time, I was simply just doing it by instinct.
And eventually, as I was working at these different jobs building roads and things like that, the contact with Goth-- my first contact with Goth was that he came by where we were working.
And apparently he didn't like the way things were going.
So-- probably too slow for his liking.
And so he lined everybody up on the-- on the assembly area.
And ordered whippings, ordered us to be whipped.
Yes, I was whipped.
I received 25 lashes with one of those whips that had these little lead weights on the end made out of leather.
Cut like a knife.
I was part of the-- the workforce and building these roads and he didn't like the way things were going.
Just--
It's hard to know.
You know, I-- I've often thought about this.
And I was wondering, you know thinking.
When I think about these things, I think about the little boy who was 13 as if he were someone else.
And as I tell it, it-- it turns out to be myself.
But it-- I think of it, and I think these guards who were ordered to whip, did they really whip me as hard as they did an adult or not?
You know, I wonder about that.

But I remember that first-- first feel of the whip was so strong that it was like being cut with a knife and my-- my reflex, the natural reflex was-- you know we were supposed to hold on to the table, there were these long tables.
And there were people being whipped on each side of these long table and to hold onto it.
And our-- our clothes were off.
You know, our pants were down.
And so the first feel of the whip, my hands just flew right back naturally.
And so the second one I got across my knuckles.
And after that, it didn't make much difference.
No.
No, you know.
I-- I-- I would give Daniel a pat on the butt, you know, to make him behave or do something that he wasn't-- you know, to stop doing something he was doing.
But I always felt funny about that.
No.
No way.
So that was my first encounter with our-- our commandant.
And I had encountered him again several times.

And finally, after I finished with this construction, the factory, the brush factor, was actually restarted in-- in Plaszow camp and I was able to get a job there, back to where I-- the same job I had when I was in the-- in ghetto.
Until the beginning of 19-- at the end of 1943-- the end of 1943 or maybe the beginning '44.
Well, Schindler had-- was able to establish a sub camp for his workers.
And my father was working at Schindler's company and my brother was with Schindler's company.
At first they were living in Plaszow and going back and forth.
But then when this sub camp was created, my brother and my father went to live in that sub camp in Schindler's company.
And my mother and I were in Plaszow.
Emalia.
Mhm.
And my sister, as you know, was still at this cable company or the electrical appliance company.
And I-- I got my brush making job back.
And my mother was still working as a cleaning lady.
And then--
Well, they were doing cleaning in the-- inside the camp.
Well, Schindler as-- after he built his sub camp decided to increase his workforce and was taking on more people.
So my father asked him to put my mother and me on the-- on the list.
As you can see, there were several Schindler's lists, you know, not-- not just one.
For example, when my brother was taken-- was added to the Schindler's company, that was another list because my father asked him to take my brother on.
And then when the next group was going to be sent to Schindler's company, then a list was created of those people who were going to be sent.

On the-- on the-- on Schindler's company I was-- I only had a number when I went on the last-- on the last leg of our trip, that is the one where we moved the company from Krakow to Plaszow because at that point, we went through a-- Gross-Rosen camp.
And they-- there we got an actual number.
Before that, all we had was a letter.
We had a letter tattooed on our arm.
K-- K-- K-- KL, I guess it was.
I used to.
I used to have it.
It just, it faded out.
Because when I first got it, I-- I sucked it out with my mouth and washed it off.
And it-- it remained on my arm all through the war, but it was very, very faint.
Nobody checked it.
And they--
Konzentrationslager.
Concentration camp.
There were Jewish doctors that tattooed us.
In any case--
I don't remember it being much-- very painful.
My-- when I was-- so Schindler agreed to--
--put my mother and me on a list and I eventually made it.

But it-- that-- that didn't go very smoothly either, because I'd already worked-- been working in this brush factory and when the Schindler company was being formed, by that time, it was obvious that it was-- it would be preferable to be in the sub camp at Schindler's company than to be in Plaszow with-- with Goth as a commandant.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] somehow someone decided that I could not be allowed out.
That I'd already-- I already had-- the pretense was that I already had a job, and I couldn't be going to Schindler's company.
But my mother was.
So I went to see her off too.
Just to-- again, same as before.
And they were forming this group, and she was going to leave.
And I almost decided that this is going-- I was going to stay in Plaszow.
But somehow, I'm not certain, but I approached the commandant or the officer who was going to escort those people to the-- to Emalia, and I reported.
I told him that I was on the list and I am not allowed to leave.

And my argument to this-- to this Nazi officer who would shoot a Jew at-- for nothing, I was explaining to him that I was not allowed out, and that my mother is in a group and she is leaving and my brother and my father are already there.
That was my argument.
Pretty naive little boy of 13 talking to this nasty looking German officer.
And I could just about-- my eye-- my eye level was just about even with his belt.
And he looked at his assistant and asked him if that was true.
And the assistant said yes.
He saw my name on the list.
So he ordered me into this group, and I made it out.
But while I was doing-- while I was in the group waiting to leave, I looked over and there was the assistant trying to talk to this officer about something about this incident.
And he was trying to explain to him why I couldn't go.
But he refused to listen.
He even got mad at his assistant for-- you know, for arguing with him.

In any case, I'd like to think that it would be nice to know that maybe for an instant this-- this Nazi, foggy, brained Nazi German who accepted Nazi philosophy that some people are less than human and they can be shot at will probably saw a little boy--
--and decided that this was the natural thing for a little boy to do is to go where his parents are.
Oh, no.
I-- I never accepted that.
You know, they had a place at the very beginning of this camp.
They had barracks just for-- for children about my age.
But I wouldn't go near those places.
I wouldn't go near those barracks.
And I always-- the whole time that I was in this situation, I tried to associate myself with adults.
Again, it was just instinct.
It was not just being that smart.
But--
I knew-- plus I knew that I could figure out that they don't value adults, able-bodied adults, why would they worry about children?
And so--
I knew that even then.
And as it turned out, they took all those children out and murdered them.
And-- there was one incident where those children were all gathered up, all the barracks and taken away in trucks and never seen again.
No.
I was-- by that time I was already in Schindler's company.
Mm.
So I was taken to Krakow where the-- where the Schindler's company was.
Emalia.
And I worked for Schindler until the company had to be dismantled because the Russians were invading in back.
And the front where the Germans was going very badly.
And the Russians were going to be in Krakow soon.
So companies like Schindler's company were just dismantle their company and let their-- their prisoners go back to Plaszow.
Yes.
Yes, we did.
I-- I used to work night shift mostly. 12 hour shifts, standing near a machine and doing labor work.
And I encountered Schindler many times as he was walking through the company.
Oh, yes, he did.
Yeah.
There was a-- there was a-- somehow-- some kind of a special relationship between Schindler and my father.
As you know, Schindler was not an angel in dealing with black-marketeering.
He was a womanizer.
And he didn't mind taking bribes and giving bribes.
And he was not your squeaky clean kind of character.
And but-- but with my father, my father having lost all his savings at the very beginning of the war, all he could offer Schindler was his skills.
And Schindler accepted that.
And I think that somehow he respected this-- this relationship so that each time my father asked him for something, he would do it.
He wasn't doing it because he-- my father had something else to offer him.
So when he asked for my mother and me to be taken on, he only listened.
And he didn't say anything.
And he had a terrific memory.
He just remembered.
And we know that it was his doing that put my mother and me on the list.
And then eventually, my sister, Aviva, who was returned from her company at about same time to Plaszow.
And she would have been just in the general population and would have had about a 100 to one chance to survive.
There were five family members, five Leysons who survived the war in Schindler's company.
At the end of the war, there were five of us there, my parents, my sister, my brother, and I.
David.
Yes.
Who actually typed it, you mean?
Yeah, those people who did the-- the accounting for him and the-- there was some Jewish accountants that did some business work for him, bookkeepers and so on, those were the people who did the list.
There weren't many children in Schindler's company.
I was-- for-- at one time, there was a person younger than I in-- in Schindler's company.
And then I was the youngest.
He was taken away, and I was the youngest in the company.
I was the youngest one working in the factory along with the adults.
Oh, yes.
Yeah, I had-- I had good-- people didn't treat me badly and-- as a-- as a child--
--not especially.
In special barracks.
Yeah, there were-- there were barracks for men and barracks for women in the same camp.
Yes.
Mm-hmm.
Yeah.
Well, you know, as-- as strange as it sounds, there-- they were.
There were people who practiced their religion, who celebrated the holidays.
As-- as hard as it was and as dangerous as it was, it was done.
Well, occasionally they would.
If they-- if they saw it, it was a very risky thing to do.
No, absolutely not.
It was all done in--
Well, Yom Kippur and Rosh Hashanah was-- was something I can remember as being kept.
In some-- in some-- in some quarters, in some situations, the sabbath.
Those people who were-- who were older than I who had practiced their religion before the war had gotten-- got together and had prayer meetings and so on.
I didn't, but--
--my father would, yeah.
Yes, absolutely.
It was always risky to do anything spiritual, whether it's singing songs or writing poems or-- or telling jokes.
You know?
And we did all of those things.
And I think that's part of resisting.
You know, resisting isn't always with mechanical weapons.
The facts are really that the Germans were conducting a war against the Jews.
And they had a special army to do this.
A special army that was equipped and had special marching orders.
And they-- their job was to kill Jews, to murder.
And we resisted and fought back with all the-- the equipment we had.
And we had men, women, and children.
So naturally, the-- the odds were against us, and we took a lot of casualties, of course.
But in the end, they didn't win the war that they wanted to win.
They actually lost that, the spiritual, the-- the people's spirit.
As hard as it was in-- in ghetto, I used to be able to-- there was a little yard and they'd put some chairs up there and tables.
And they had a cabaret, a stage production.
You know, people were doing stage work.
And I used to chin myself up on the back of the wall to see what they were doing.
And there'd be a stand up comedian telling jokes, sad jokes, you know, kind of gallows type, but nevertheless, it was-- it was going on all the time.
